steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank falls between the independent clause ending in "century" and the word "revealing," which begins a participial phrase modifying the whole preceding clause. The question tests whether you can correctly punctuate the boundary between an independent clause and a trailing participial phrase.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "century. Revealing": A period creates two separate sentences. "Revealing how the personal and political authority..." is a participial phrase, not a complete sentence — it has no subject or finite verb. A period here produces a fragment. ✗
  • "century; revealing": A semicolon connects two independent clauses. The material after the semicolon is a participial phrase, not an independent clause, so a semicolon is grammatically incorrect here. ✗
  • "century, revealing": A comma correctly sets off a trailing participial phrase from the independent clause it modifies. This follows standard English convention. ✓
  • "century revealing": No punctuation between an independent clause and a trailing participial phrase is not standard. The participial phrase must be set off by a comma to signal that it is supplementary information modifying the clause, not part of the core sentence structure. ✗
That matches the correct answer — C is correct.
